entry.title: A Manifesto Against Pseudo-Intellectualism — On the Most Dangerous People in the Room

link.title: A Manifesto Against Pseudo-Intellectualism — On the Most Dangerous People in the Room

entry.content:
My favorite word in English is “pseudo”. I seriously love it. You can put it in front of anything and it just completely messes up the meaning. Anyway…

I want to talk about the most dangerous people in contemporary intellectual life.

Not the ignorant. The ignorant are not dangerous — they know they don’t know, or they don’t know they don’t know, but in either case they are not actively doing damage to the infrastructure of knowledge.

Not the stupid. Stupidity is a cognitive limitation, not a moral failure. You cannot hold someone responsible for the architecture of their brain.

I want to talk about the pseudo-intellectual.

The person who has received education — sometimes excellent education — and emerged from it not with knowledge but with the performance of knowledge.

The person who has learned not how to think but how to sound like they think.

The person who deploys vocabulary as camouflage, citations as authority, complexity as a substitute for clarity, and the appearance of rigor as a replacement for actual rigor.

This person is dangerous in a way that ignorance never is — because they are convincing. Because they have credentials. Because they sit on committees and write reports and give talks and shape policy and teach students and review papers and make decisions that affect real people’s real lives —

while understanding, at a fundamental level, almost nothing about what they are doing.

I. The Taxonomy

Let me be precise about what I mean — because “pseudo-intellectual” is often used as a simple insult, and I want to use it as a diagnosis.

There are several distinct varieties.

The Credential Hoarder.

Collects degrees, affiliations, publications — not as byproducts of genuine inquiry but as the goal itself.

The CV is the intellectual life. The paper is the thought. The conference is the work.

They have learned to produce the outputs of intellectual labor — the paper, the grant application, the review, the talk — without the inputs.

Without genuine curiosity. Without the sustained encounter with difficulty. Without the willingness to be wrong.

The credential hoarder’s papers cite correctly. The methodology section is technically adequate. The conclusions are hedged appropriately.

And the work contributes nothing — because nothing was genuinely asked, genuinely investigated, genuinely risked.

The Jargon Welder.

Has learned that complexity signals intelligence.

Writes sentences like: “The heteronormative discursive formations that undergird the epistemological violence of neoliberal subjectification require a transgressive praxis of intersectional counter-hegemonic resistance.”

This sentence means something — or rather, it could mean something — in the right context, with the right interlocutor, with genuine analytical work beneath it.

Usually it means nothing.

It is a performance of having thought about a subject without the inconvenience of actually thinking about it.

The jargon welder uses complexity not to illuminate but to intimidate — to make the reader feel that if they do not understand, it is their failure, not the writer’s.

This is the inversion of intellectual virtue.

Good writing makes the difficult clear. Bad writing makes the simple obscure — and calls the obscurity depth.

The Reference Dropper.

Has read — or skimmed, or read about — the important texts.

Drops names with confidence: Foucault, Derrida, Lacan, Žižek, Agamben. Or: Kahneman, Taleb, Gladwell, Peterson. Or: Feynman, Hawking, Penrose, Tegmark.

The references are real. The understanding is not.

The reference dropper has learned that Discipline and Punish is about power, that The Black Swan is about tail risk, that A Brief History of Time is about cosmology.

They have not read these books carefully. They have not tested their understanding by trying to explain the arguments to someone who has also read them carefully.

They have not asked: does this argument actually work? What are its assumptions? Its limits? Where does it fail?

They have acquired the brand without the product.

The Confident Generalist.

Has opinions on everything. Strong opinions. Stated confidently.

Speaks with authority on quantum mechanics, political economy, evolutionary psychology, the history of the Ottoman Empire, the neuroscience of addiction, the future of artificial intelligence.

Has thought seriously about none of them.

The confident generalist has learned that the appearance of confidence is socially rewarded — that hesitation reads as weakness, that “I don’t know” reads as ignorance, that the person who speaks first and loudest is perceived as the most knowledgeable.

They are exploiting a genuine cognitive bias — the Dunning-Kruger effect — but from the inside, as a strategy.

They know just enough to be convincing. They do not know enough to know what they do not know.

The Ideological Ventriloquist.

Has replaced thought with tribe.

All questions have predetermined answers — determined by political affiliation, disciplinary loyalty, institutional interest, or personal identity.

They do not investigate. They confirm. They do not argue. They signal. They do not change their minds — because their minds were never genuinely engaged in the first place.

The ideological ventriloquist is particularly dangerous in academia and in policy — where they occupy positions designed for genuine inquiry and use them for tribal warfare.

II. Why They Are Dangerous

You might ask: isn’t this just vanity? Aren’t they harmless pretenders, whose bluster affects nothing important?

No.

They occupy space.

Academic positions, editorial boards, policy committees, expert panels — these are finite resources. A pseudo-intellectual in a position of intellectual authority displaces a genuine thinker.

The mediocre paper published is the good paper rejected. The confident fool on the committee is the careful thinker silenced.

They corrupt standards.

When pseudo-intellectual work is rewarded — published, cited, funded — it signals to everyone in the system what the system values.

It trains the next generation in the production of the performance of thought rather than thought itself.

It creates a race to the bottom of credentialed mediocrity.

They make consequential decisions.

The pseudo-intellectual is not contained in the seminar room.

They write the reports that shape policy. They review the drugs that reach patients. They evaluate the technologies that are deployed. They advise the governments that make decisions affecting millions of people.

Their confident wrongness — their inability to know what they do not know — is not an academic problem.

It is a public health problem. It is a political problem. It is a civilizational problem.

They undermine trust in expertise.

This is perhaps the most damaging effect.

When pseudo-intellectuals speak with authority — and are wrong — and are later revealed to be wrong — the damage does not fall only on them.

It falls on expertise itself.

The person who trusted the confident generalist and was burned by their confident wrongness does not conclude: “I should find better experts.”

They conclude: “Experts cannot be trusted.”

This is how pseudo-intellectualism feeds anti-intellectualism.

The fake expert and the expert-denier are not opposites. They are collaborators — each making the other’s position possible.

III. The Difference — What Genuine Thinking Looks Like

It is not enough to diagnose the disease. We need to describe the health.

What distinguishes genuine intellectual work from its simulation?

Genuine thinking is uncomfortable.

Real inquiry goes places you did not expect. It produces conclusions you did not want. It requires you to revise beliefs you held firmly.

If your thinking never makes you uncomfortable — if you always end up where you expected — you are not thinking. You are confirming.

The pseudo-intellectual’s thinking is always comfortable because it is not thinking. It is performance — and the performance is rehearsed.

Genuine thinking fails publicly.

Real intellectual work includes wrong hypotheses, dead ends, failed experiments, retracted papers, revised positions.

The willingness to be publicly wrong — to say I was wrong, here is why, here is what I now think — is one of the clearest markers of intellectual honesty.

The pseudo-intellectual is never wrong — or never admits it — because they were never genuinely committed to a position that could be falsified. Their positions are held for social reasons. Social reasons do not require falsification.

Genuine thinking has limits.

The genuine expert knows the edge of their expertise.

They know which questions they can answer, which they cannot, which they could answer with more work, and which are genuinely open.

They say I don’t know without shame.

“I don’t know” is the most important phrase in intellectual life.

It is also the phrase the pseudo-intellectual can never say — because not knowing is not part of the performance.

Genuine thinking is slow.

Real understanding takes time. Reading carefully. Thinking between readings. Returning to texts. Changing your mind. Sitting with difficulty.

The pseudo-intellectual is always fast — because they are not building understanding, they are assembling presentation.

Presentation can be assembled quickly. Understanding cannot be.

IV. The Institutional Enablers

The pseudo-intellectual does not appear in a vacuum. They are produced and sustained by institutions that have lost sight of what they are for.

The publish-or-perish system rewards quantity over quality, the production of papers over the production of ideas. It creates systematic pressure to produce the appearance of research rather than research itself.

The grant system rewards legibility over importance — the ability to describe your work in terms that a committee will understand and fund, rather than the work that most needs doing.

It rewards the already-established over the genuinely new. It rewards the safe over the risky. It rewards the pseudo-intellectual who knows how to write a grant application over the genuine thinker who has not learned that particular performance.

The conference system rewards presence over substance — the ability to network, to be seen, to deliver a polished twenty-minute talk over the ability to think deeply about a problem over years.

The peer review system has been corrupted in many fields by the same dynamics — reviewers who are not experts in the work they review, who evaluate papers by surface features rather than by genuine engagement with the argument.

These are systemic failures. They produce pseudo-intellectualism at scale.

Fixing them requires institutional courage — the willingness to reward genuine inquiry over productive output, honest failure over performed success, the long slow work of thinking over the rapid production of credentials.

V. The Self-Examination

Here is the hardest part.

Everything I have said about the pseudo-intellectual applies, at different times and in different ways, to every person who has been educated — including me. Including you.

We all perform. We all sometimes deploy vocabulary we have not earned. We all sometimes speak with confidence that exceeds our knowledge. We all sometimes avoid the discomfort of genuine intellectual engagement.

The difference between the pseudo-intellectual and the genuine thinker is not that the genuine thinker never performs.

It is that the genuine thinker knows when they are performing — and is troubled by it — and returns, as often as possible, to the harder work of actually thinking.

The Socratic legacy is precisely this: the recognition that the examined life requires constant examination — not of others, but of oneself.

“The unexamined life is not worth living.”

The unexamined intellectual performance is not worth giving.

Ask yourself:

Do I actually understand this, or do I understand how to talk about it?

Could I explain this argument to a genuinely intelligent person who has never encountered it — explain it so they understand it, not so they are impressed by my familiarity with it?

If someone pushed back on my position — if someone found a genuine flaw — would I be able to engage with the flaw, or would I retreat into jargon?

Have I changed my mind about something important in the last year? The last five years? If not — am I thinking, or confirming?

These questions are uncomfortable. They are supposed to be.

VI. What We Owe Each Other

There is a moral dimension here that I want to state directly.

Knowledge is a public good.

When you claim expertise you do not have — when you occupy an intellectual position under false pretenses — you are not just embarrassing yourself.

You are stealing from the public.

You are taking the social trust that is extended to experts — trust built up over generations by people who did the hard work, who were genuinely right about important things, who earned the authority of expertise —

and spending it on your performance.

Every confident wrong pronouncement by someone with impressive credentials depletes that trust.

We are already in a trust crisis. We are already in a moment where expertise is distrusted — often wrongly, but not always wrongly — by large portions of the population.

The pseudo-intellectual is one of the causes of this crisis.

Not the only cause. Not the primary cause. But a real cause.

You owe the truth to the people who trust you with their attention, their credulity, their decisions.

You owe them your genuine best thinking — not your polished performance of thinking.

If you don’t have genuine thinking to offer — if you have not done the work — then the most intellectually honest thing you can do is silence.

“Whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent.”

Wittgenstein said this about metaphysics. It applies equally to expertise.

If you do not know — if you have not done the work — be silent.

Or say: I don’t know.

These are not failures. They are the beginning of honesty. And honesty is the beginning of thought.

VII. The Standard

I want to end with what I am for, not just what I am against.

I am for the person who reads a paper and asks: does this argument actually work?

I am for the person who encounters a new idea and sits with the discomfort of not yet understanding it — rather than immediately assimilating it into what they already know.

I am for the person who changes their mind when the evidence changes — not because changing your mind is fashionable, but because it is what thinking requires.

I am for the person who says I don’t know without shame — and means it as an invitation to find out, not as the end of the conversation.

I am for the person who is more interested in being right than in appearing right.

I am for the slow thinker — the person who takes years to write a book because the ideas required years to develop.

I am for the careful reader — the person who reads slowly, who marks up the margins, who returns to difficult passages, who argues with the author in their head.

I am for the honest failure — the person who publishes the result that contradicts their hypothesis, who reports the null finding, who says: we were wrong, here is what we learned.

I am for genuine intellectual humility — not the performed humility of the person who says of course I could be wrong and then proceeds as if they cannot be — but the lived humility of the person who has been wrong enough times to know it can always happen again.

This is not a low standard.

It is the only standard that deserves the name intellectual.

Coda

“The first step to knowledge is knowing what you do not know.”

This is attributed to various people. The attribution is uncertain. The content is Socratic.

The pseudo-intellectual knows everything. That is how you know they know nothing.

The genuine thinker knows what they don’t know — and spends their life, carefully, reducing the territory of ignorance.

Not eliminating it. Reducing it.

The territory of ignorance is infinite. The genuine thinker is at peace with this.

The pseudo-intellectual cannot afford to be.

This is a manifesto, not a verdict. Including against myself. On the days when I perform rather than think. Which is more days than I would like.
entry.summary: Not a polite essay. A reckoning with the people who weaponize the appearance of knowledge while being its most committed enemies.

entry.title: Seneca — On Time, Death, and the Art of Living Before You Die

link.title: Seneca — On Time, Death, and the Art of Living Before You Die

entry.content:
“Omnia, Lucili, aliena sunt, tempus tantum nostrum est.”

Everything, Lucilius, belongs to others. Time alone is ours.

Seneca wrote this in a letter. He was an old man. He had been one of the most powerful men in Rome — tutor and advisor to Nero, the most feared emperor in history. He had been extraordinarily wealthy. He had been exiled, recalled, celebrated, suspected.

And he sat down and wrote to his friend Lucilius: time alone is ours.

Not the palace. Not the money. Not the influence. Not the books or the reputation or the name.

Time.

And then, immediately, the devastating follow-up:

“Yet such is the ebb and flow of fortune that we allow time — the one thing that is truly ours — to be taken from us.”

I. The Man

Lucius Annaeus Seneca was born around 4 BCE in Córdoba, in Roman Spain.

He came to Rome as a child, studied rhetoric and philosophy, became a brilliant advocate and writer, and rose to prominence under the emperor Claudius.

Then Claudius’s wife accused him of adultery with the emperor’s niece. He was exiled to Corsica for eight years.

He was recalled by Agrippina — Claudius’s new wife, Nero’s mother — to serve as tutor to her son.

When Nero became emperor at sixteen, Seneca was his most important advisor. For several years, the partnership worked — Seneca’s moderation restraining the emperor’s cruelty.

Then it didn’t.

Nero became what Nero became. Seneca retreated from public life — tried to give back his wealth, tried to disengage from the court.

In 65 CE, Nero accused him of involvement in the Pisonian conspiracy — a plot to assassinate the emperor. The evidence was thin. It didn’t matter.

Nero sent soldiers to Seneca’s villa with an order to die.

Seneca opened his veins.

He died slowly — the veins of an old man do not bleed quickly. He took poison; it didn’t work. He was placed in a hot bath to hasten the bleeding.

He dictated to scribes until he could no longer speak.

He died as he had written — discussing philosophy in the face of death, practicing what he had preached in letter after letter, essay after essay, for thirty years.

II. On the Shortness of Life

De Brevitate Vitae — On the Shortness of Life — is perhaps the most important thing Seneca wrote.

It begins with a paradox:

“It is not that we have a short time to live, but that we waste a great deal of it.”

Life is not short. Life is long enough — more than long enough — if used well.

The problem is not the quantity of time. It is what we do with it.

We give our time to everyone who asks. We guard our money carefully — we are appalled if someone wastes our property — but we let people waste our hours, our days, our years, without protest, without even noticing.

“People are frugal in guarding their personal property; but as soon as it comes to squandering time they are most wasteful of the one thing in which it is right to be stingy.”

Seneca identifies the thieves of time:

The ambitious — always preparing to live, always arranging the conditions under which life will begin. First I need this promotion. Then this house. Then this security. Then I will begin to live.

The pleasure-seekers — always consuming, never present, always moving to the next experience.

The distracted — pulled in every direction by other people’s urgencies, never attending to their own.

The greedy — spending their life accumulating what life cannot be lived without spending.

All of them, Seneca says, are dying badly — not because death is near but because they are not living.

“They lose the day in expectation of the night, and the night in fear of the dawn.”

III. The Letters to Lucilius

The Epistulae Morales — Moral Letters to Lucilius — are Seneca’s masterpiece.

One hundred and twenty-four letters, written in the last years of his life, to his friend Gaius Lucilius Junior — a man younger than Seneca, a government official in Sicily, who had apparently asked Seneca to help him live better.

The letters are not treatises. They are not systematic philosophy. They are the most intimate philosophical document from the ancient world — a man thinking out loud, revising himself, admitting uncertainty, sharing what he has learned and what he has failed to learn.

They begin, famously:

“Ita fac, mi Lucili: vindica te tibi.”

Do this, my Lucilius: claim yourself for yourself.

The first letter. The first sentence. Already everything is there.

Claim yourself for yourself — take back the time that has been dispersed, the attention that has been scattered, the self that has been given away in small pieces to everyone who asked.

Each letter takes up a theme — friendship, travel, the fear of death, anger, grief, luxury, poverty, solitude, crowds — and works it through with a combination of personal reflection, philosophical argument, and the occasional devastating aphorism that lodges in the memory and will not leave.

IV. On Friendship

Seneca’s letters are, above all, about friendship.

Not friendship as a social category — as acquaintance, as usefulness, as pleasant company.

Friendship as the deepest form of human connection — the relationship in which another person is genuinely known and genuinely valued.

“Hoc primum philosophia promittit: sensum communem, humanitatem et congregationem.”

“Philosophy promises above all: common sense, humanity, and fellowship.”

And more directly:

“Sine amico nemo tutus vivere potest.”

“Without a friend, no one can live securely.”

But Seneca is precise about what friendship requires:

“Before you make a friend, judge whether he is worthy to be made a friend. After you have made a friend, trust him with everything.”

The order matters. Judgment first. Then total trust. There is no friendship without judgment — the assessment of character, of values, of reliability. And there is no friendship with only judgment — the trust must be complete, or it is not friendship.

He also says something that feels almost modern in its psychological precision:

“Recede in te ipse quantum potes.”

“Retire into yourself as much as you can.”

Solitude and friendship are not opposites for Seneca. They are complementary disciplines. You must know yourself before you can truly know another. And you must know another to truly know yourself.

The friend is a mirror — not a flattering one, but an honest one.

“Refer everything to a good person who you would want to imitate. Live as if he is watching.”

V. On Death

Seneca returns to death in almost every letter.

Not morbidly — not with the fascination of depression — but with the insistence of a man who has understood that the refusal to think about death is the primary source of the wasted life.

“Omnia, Lucili, aliena sunt, tempus tantum nostrum est.”

Time is ours — and it runs out. To not know this — to live as if you will not die — is to be unable to choose.

You cannot choose well what to do with your life if you do not know how much of it remains. You cannot prioritize correctly if you believe there will always be more time.

“Cogita quantum temporis absumpserit.”

“Think how much time has already been consumed.”

Not to produce guilt — but to produce clarity.

The Stoic exercise of memento mori — remember that you will die — is not pessimism. It is the most practical philosophical tool Seneca describes: the practice of holding death in mind as the clarifying horizon against which all choices become legible.

What matters, if today were the last day? What would you stop doing? What would you start?

“Dum differtur vita transcurrit.”

“While we are postponing, life speeds by.”

And his most personal formulation — written, perhaps, when the soldiers were already on their way:

“Omnia, Lucili, aliena sunt, tempus tantum nostrum est. In hoc unum avarus esse licet.”

“Everything belongs to others, Lucilius; time alone is ours. In this one thing it is permitted to be a miser.”

Be a miser with time. Give it to no one carelessly. Spend it on nothing that does not deserve it.

VI. The Stoic Framework

Seneca was a Stoic — part of the philosophical school founded by Zeno of Citium in Athens around 300 BCE.

But he was an unusual Stoic — the most human of them, the most willing to admit difficulty, the most honest about the gap between what philosophy teaches and what a person actually does.

The Stoic framework:

The dichotomy of control: Some things are up to us — our judgments, desires, aversions. Some things are not — our body, reputation, wealth, other people.

Unhappiness comes from wanting what is not up to us. Freedom comes from wanting only what is up to us.

The discipline of desire: Desire only what virtue requires. Prefer — but do not require — health, wealth, friendship. Be indifferent — in the technical sense — to everything external.

Living according to nature: The rational animal’s nature is reason. To live well is to live rationally — in accordance with the logos, the rational principle that structures reality.

Seneca accepted all of this. He also accepted that he often failed to live it.

This honesty is what distinguishes him from the more austere Stoics — Epictetus, who was enslaved and never complained, Marcus Aurelius, who ruled an empire and wrote his private doubts in a notebook.

Seneca was wealthy, powerful, sometimes hypocritical — he wrote about the dangers of wealth while being extraordinarily rich. He wrote about the importance of not fearing death while apparently fearing it.

His critics — then and now — point this out.

His response, in one of the letters:

“I am not a wise man, nor shall I ever be one. Demand of me not that I be equal to the best, but that I be better than the wicked. I am satisfied if I subtract something from my vices each day.”

Not perfection. Improvement. Not the ideal Stoic sage — a figure even the Stoics admitted was theoretical. A man trying, imperfectly, daily, to do better.

This is what makes him readable across two thousand years.

VII. The Consolations

Seneca wrote three formal consolations — letters to people in grief — that are among the most moving prose of the ancient world.

To Marcia — a woman who had lost her son. To Helvia — his own mother, written during his exile. To Polybius — a freedman at Claudius’s court, who had lost his brother.

The Ad Helviam — to his mother — is extraordinary.

He has been exiled. He is in Corsica — far from Rome, far from his work, far from his friends. And he writes to his mother not to receive consolation but to give it.

“I have decided, dearest mother, to conquer my grief rather than to cheat it.”

He does not pretend the exile doesn’t hurt. He does not perform Stoic indifference. He acknowledges the pain — his pain, her pain — and then works, carefully, philosophically, to find the resources within that pain for endurance.

The resources he finds: the constancy of virtue, which cannot be exiled. The life of the mind, which no location can confine. The friendship of philosophy, which travels everywhere. The love between mother and son, which distance cannot touch.

“Wherever I turn, I see the proofs of your affection.”

This is Stoicism with a human face — not the denial of feeling but its transformation through honest philosophical attention.

VIII. Seneca and Power

The most difficult question about Seneca is the one his critics have always asked:

How do you square the philosophy with the life?

He wrote about the dangers of wealth — and was fabulously wealthy. He wrote about the importance of virtue — and served a murderer. He wrote about the shortness of life — and spent much of it in the service of Nero’s court.

Did he know what Nero was? Almost certainly. Did he stay because he believed he could moderate the evil? Perhaps. For a time. Did he stay too long? Almost certainly.

The charge of hypocrisy is serious. Philosophy that cannot be lived is not philosophy — it is literature.

Seneca’s answer, from the letters:

“Do not look at who says this — look at what is said.”

The teaching stands independent of the teacher. The truth of omnia aliena sunt, tempus tantum nostrum est does not depend on whether Seneca spent his own time well.

This is both a defense and an evasion. It is also, in a certain limited sense, right.

The letters are true whether Seneca lived them or not. But they would be truer if he had. And we should hold both truths simultaneously — the wisdom of the letters and the failure of the life — without letting either cancel the other.

This is what it means to read a philosopher honestly.

IX. The Death

When the soldiers came, Seneca was ready.

Or as ready as a man can be who has spent decades preparing and still, in the end, is a body that bleeds slowly.

He tried to comfort his wife, Paulina, who wanted to die with him. He asked her to live.

“I showed you ways of looking after life; you prefer the glory of dying. I will not begrudge you such a noble example. Let the fortitude of this our common end be alike — but let there be more in your departure that is worthy of praise.”

She survived. Nero ordered it. She lived many years after. The scars on her wrists marked her for the rest of her life.

Seneca dictated his last words. We do not know exactly what they were — Tacitus says they were spoken to friends and scribes — but he died speaking.

Still thinking. Still in the letters.

X. What He Gives Us

Seneca gives us something that most philosophers do not:

The permission to be imperfect while still trying.

Not the Platonic philosopher-king who has seen the sun. Not the Zen master who has achieved emptiness. Not the perfect Stoic sage who is indifferent to everything external.

A man. Brilliant, flawed, compromised, trying. Writing letters to a friend about how to live better.

“Nusquam est qui ubique est.”

“One who is everywhere is nowhere.”

“Recede in te ipse quantum potes.”

“Retire into yourself as much as you can.”

“Ita fac, mi Lucili: vindica te tibi.”

“Claim yourself for yourself.”

Two thousand years old. Written this morning. Written for you.

Further reading: Seneca — Letters from a Stoic (trans. Robin Campbell — Penguin Classics) Seneca — On the Shortness of Life (trans. C.D.N. Costa) Seneca — Dialogues and Essays (trans. John Davie — Oxford World’s Classics) For context: Paul Veyne — Seneca: The Life of a Stoic (2003) For the Stoic framework: Marcus Aurelius — Meditations (trans. Gregory Hays)
entry.summary: The most human of the Stoics — on wasted time, genuine friendship, and why we are all dying badly.

entry.title: Hesiod, the Theogony, and Our Oedipal Wounds — On Myths That Know Us Better Than We Know Ourselves

link.title: Hesiod, the Theogony, and Our Oedipal Wounds — On Myths That Know Us Better Than We Know Ourselves

entry.content:
Before there was anything, there was Chaos.

Not disorder — the word means something more precise, something closer to the primordial gap, the yawning abyss, the opened mouth of nothingness.

Χάος — Kháos.

From this opening, everything comes.

Gaia — the deep-breasted earth.

Tartarus — the dark pit beneath the earth.

Eros — desire, the force that makes things reach toward each other.

Erebus — darkness. Night.

And from Night: Day.

From Darkness: Space.

This is how Hesiod begins. Not with a god who creates. With a gap that opens — and from the opening, generation.

The universe does not begin with intention. It begins with absence that cannot sustain itself, that generates in order to fill itself, that produces the world as a consequence of its own incompleteness.

This is, I want to argue, not only a cosmogony.

It is the deepest description we have of what it is to be human.

I. Hesiod and the Theogony

Hesiod was a Greek poet — contemporary with Homer, perhaps slightly later, perhaps slightly earlier.

He came from Ascra, in Boeotia — a small farming village, not the heroic world of the Homeric epics.

Where Homer wrote about warriors and kings — the world of kleos, glory, the great deed — Hesiod wrote about farmers, laborers, the grinding difficulty of ordinary life.

He wrote two major poems that survive:

Works and Days — a poem of practical instruction, addressed to his brother Perses, about farming, justice, and the rhythms of the year.

Theogony — the birth of the gods, the genealogy of the cosmos, the account of how everything came to be.

The Theogony is our source for the systematic Greek cosmogony — the account of origins that shaped Western thought from the pre-Socratics through Plato through Ovid through Milton through Freud.

It is approximately a thousand lines. It is the founding document of Western mythology.

II. The Violence at the Origin

What the Theogony tells us, relentlessly, is that creation requires violence.

Not violence as accident. Violence as structure.

Ouranos — Sky — mates with Gaia — Earth — but refuses to let his children be born. He pushes them back into their mother as they emerge.

Gaia, in agony, fashions a great sickle and asks her children to castrate their father.

Only Kronos agrees.

He castrates Ouranos. The severed genitals fall into the sea. From the foam — aphros — that gathers around them, Aphrodite is born.

From the blood that falls on the earth: the Giants, the Furies, the ash-tree Nymphs.

Creation from dismemberment. Beauty from castration. The goddess of love born from an act of violence.

This is not incidental. It is the Theogony’s central argument:

The world as it is required the destruction of what was.

Every generation of gods comes to power by overthrowing the previous generation.

Ouranos is castrated by Kronos. Kronos swallows his own children to prevent them from overthrowing him — because Ouranos and Gaia have prophesied that he will be overthrown by his son.

Zeus, hidden by his mother Rhea, forces Kronos to vomit up his siblings, defeats the Titans in the Titanomachy, and establishes the current order.

The current order — our world, the world we inhabit — is built on a foundation of successive violations.

Father castrated by son. Children swallowed by father. Father defeated by son.

This is not just myth. It is the structure of time itself — the new requiring the destruction of the old, the future feeding on the past, every generation haunted by what it had to do to become itself.

III. Kronos and the Devouring Father

Kronos swallows his children.

Hestia. Demeter. Hera. Hades. Poseidon. Each one, as born, taken from Rhea’s arms and swallowed.

Why?

Because the oracle said his son would overthrow him — as he overthrew his father.

He knows what he is. He knows what he did. He knows what will be done to him.

And so he swallows his children — not from malice, but from fear. From the terror of the inevitable.

This is the Theogony’s most psychologically precise moment.

The father who destroys his children is not a monster. He is a man who knows history — who knows that the structure of time requires his replacement — and who cannot bear it.

He swallows them to stop time. To prevent the future from happening. To remain the sovereign of the present by eliminating those who would make him the past.

It does not work.

It never works.

Zeus is hidden. Zeus grows. Zeus returns. The swallowed children are vomited up. Kronos is defeated.

The future cannot be swallowed. It can only be delayed — and the delay makes it more violent when it finally comes.

IV. Zeus and the New Order

Zeus wins.

He defeats the Titans. He defeats Typhon. He establishes the Olympian order — assigns domains to gods, marries Hera, produces an extraordinary number of children through an extraordinary number of liaisons.

Zeus is not a gentle god.

He rules through power — through the thunderbolt, through the threat of force, through the reminder that he has defeated everyone who challenged him.

But he also rules through intelligence — mêtis — a quality so important that he swallows the goddess Metis when she is pregnant with Athena, because the oracle says that Metis will bear a child greater than the father.

He cannot let that happen. So he swallows Metis — and Athena is born from his head, fully formed, in armor.

Wisdom born from the inside of the sovereign’s skull. Intelligence that belongs entirely to power — that cannot exist outside it, that is born through the sovereign’s self-cannibalism.

Notice the repetition:

Ouranos prevents birth. Kronos swallows children. Zeus swallows the mother of his most important child.

Each generation finds a new way to try to stop the next generation.

Each fails.

Because the structure of time cannot be stopped — only deformed.

V. The Oedipal Structure

Freud named his most famous complex after Oedipus.

This has been so thoroughly absorbed into culture that we have forgotten how strange it is — how much it both illuminates and distorts what Sophocles actually wrote.

Freud’s Oedipus complex: the child desires the parent of the opposite sex, fears castration (or its equivalent) from the same-sex parent, and eventually resolves this through identification with the same-sex parent and renunciation of the desire.

This is a story about desire and its renunciation — about the child’s entry into the social order through the abandonment of an impossible wish.

Sophocles’s Oedipus is something different.

Oedipus kills his father and sleeps with his mother — but he does not know it.

The horror of Oedipus Rex is not desire. It is knowledge.

Oedipus is the man who will not stop asking.

Warned by Tiresias to stop the investigation — told, effectively, that the answer will destroy him — he continues.

“I will not listen. I must know the truth.”

He finds out. He knows. He puts out his own eyes.

Why does he blind himself?

Not as punishment for the incest — which was unknowing — but because the eyes that saw but could not see are no longer worthy of the light.

He was blind when he could see. Now he will be blind without pretense.

VI. Oedipus as Epistemological Hero

This is the reading that mythology gives us that psychology obscures:

Oedipus is not primarily about desire. He is about the will to know — and what knowing costs.

He is the detective — the first detective in Western literature — who will not stop investigating even when the investigation leads to himself.

He is the scientist who will not falsify data to protect a comfortable conclusion.

He is the philosopher who follows the argument wherever it leads — including to the place where it destroys the philosopher’s own foundations.

“I must know the truth” — this is not hubris in the conventional sense. It is the deepest form of intellectual courage: the willingness to be the object of your own investigation, the refusal to grant yourself exemption from the questions you ask of others.

Oedipus is destroyed by truth. He would not have had it otherwise.

There is something in this that resonates beyond Greek tragedy.

Every serious intellectual — every person who has followed an argument to its uncomfortable conclusion, who has found themselves implicated in what they set out to investigate, who has discovered that the questioner is part of the question — has been Oedipus.

The willingness to put out your own eyes rather than unsee what you have seen — this is not pathology. It is the highest form of intellectual honesty.

VII. The Hesiodic and the Oedipal — Together

What connects Hesiod’s cosmogony to Sophocles’s tragedy?

Both are stories about the violence of origins — about what must be destroyed for anything to come into being.

In Hesiod: the cosmos requires castration, swallowing, defeat. The world as it is is built on the ruins of what it destroyed to become itself.

In Sophocles: knowledge requires the destruction of the self that did not know. The Oedipus who knows at the end of the play is not the Oedipus who began it. He has been destroyed and remade — at the cost of his eyes, his throne, his family, his city.

Both stories say: you cannot become without destruction.

The cosmos cannot be born without the father being unmade.

The truth cannot be known without the knower being transformed by what they learn.

This is the deepest mythological wisdom:

Origin is violence. Knowledge is violence. Becoming is violence — not in the sense of cruelty, but in the sense of irreversibility.

What has been destroyed to make you cannot be restored.

The eyes that saw Ouranos castrated — the ears that heard the oracle — cannot unremember.

The only question is: what do you do with the irreversibility?

VIII. The Mythological Answer

Greek mythology does not offer comfort.

It does not say: the violence was worth it. It does not say: everything happens for a reason. It does not say: the suffering will be redeemed.

It says: this is the structure of things.

Chaos opens. Generation begins. Each generation destroys and is destroyed. The cosmos is built on castration. Knowledge is built on self-blinding. The order we inhabit is the product of violences we did not choose and cannot undo.

And we live here. In this order. With these origins. Carrying these wounds.

The Theogony does not ask us to overcome our origins. It asks us to know them.

Oedipus does not ask us to avoid the truth. It asks us to follow it — wherever it leads — including into darkness.

This is the mythological answer:

Not redemption. Recognition.

The courageous act is not to transcend the violence at the origin of things. It is to look at it clearly — with eyes that have earned their sight through the willingness to see — and say:

Yes. This is where I come from. This is what made me. I know it now. I will not look away.

IX. What We Carry

We are all Oedipal in the mythological sense — not in Freud’s narrower sense, but in the sense that Sophocles intended:

We are all the products of origins we did not choose and cannot fully know.

We are all carrying wounds inflicted before we could consent to them — family wounds, cultural wounds, historical wounds, the wounds of being born into a specific time and place and body.

And we are all, like Oedipus, confronted with the choice:

To stop asking. To accept the comfortable not-knowing. To live in the city of Thebes without looking too closely at how it was built.

Or to ask. To follow the question. To risk what Oedipus risked — the discovery that the investigator is implicated in what they investigate, that the wound and the healer are the same person, that knowing changes nothing about what happened and everything about who you are in relation to what happened.

Tiresias warned Oedipus. He did not listen.

I think Sophocles admired him for it.

I do too.

X. Hesiod’s Last Word

The Theogony ends not with resolution but with catalogue — the endless list of gods and their children, the proliferation of divine genealogy that is the cosmos’s way of sustaining itself through multiplication.

There is something honest about this ending.

The cosmos does not resolve. It generates. It continues. It produces more of itself — more gods, more heroes, more stories, more attempts to understand the original gap that opened before anything was.

Chaos — the first word. The gap that is not nothing but the condition of everything.

We come from this.

Not from order, not from intention, not from a god who shaped us with a clear purpose in mind —

but from an opening, a gap, an abyss that could not sustain its own emptiness and began, from that unbearable fullness of nothing, to generate.

We are the cosmos knowing itself.

Imperfectly. Painfully. With eyes that sometimes need to be put out before they can truly see.

But knowing.

That is what we are. That is what we do.

That is, perhaps, enough.

Further reading: Hesiod — Theogony and Works and Days (trans. M.L. West — Oxford World’s Classics) Sophocles — The Three Theban Plays (trans. Robert Fagles — Penguin Classics) Walter Burkert — Greek Religion (1985) — the essential scholarly introduction Jean-Pierre Vernant — Myth and Thought Among the Greeks (1983) — the best structural analysis of Greek mythology Bernard Knox — The Heroic Temper (1964) — on Oedipus and the will to know Roberto Calasso — The Marriage of Cadmus and Harmony (1993) — mythology as literature; the most beautiful book about Greek myth
entry.summary: On Hesiod's cosmogony, the violence at the origin of things, and why the myth of Oedipus is not about incest but about the unbearable desire to know.

entry.title: What Is Time? — Augustine, Heidegger, and the Physics of the Passing Moment

link.title: What Is Time? — Augustine, Heidegger, and the Physics of the Passing Moment

entry.content:
Augustine of Hippo, in the year 397, wrote the most honest sentence in the history of philosophy:

“Quid est ergo tempus? Si nemo ex me quaerat, scio; si quaerenti explicare velim, nescio.”

“What then is time? If no one asks me, I know. If I want to explain it to someone who asks, I do not know.”

He wrote this in the Confessions — a book that is itself a meditation on time, on memory, on the self that persists through the passing of moments.

He is right.

You know what time is in the way you know how to breathe — without thinking about it, without being able to explain it.

The moment you try to explain it, the thing dissolves.

The present: where is it? By the time you name it, it is gone. The past: it no longer exists. The future: it does not yet exist.

And yet time is the most real thing there is. More real than space. More intimate than thought.

You are inside it right now. You cannot get out. You cannot examine it from outside.

This is the problem of time.

I. Augustine — Time as the Soul’s Extension

Augustine’s analysis of time in Book XI of the Confessions is the first great philosophical account of temporal experience.

His starting point: the cosmological question.

“What was God doing before the Creation?”

The naive answer: preparing hell for those who ask such questions. Augustine quotes this with gentle irony.

His real answer: the question is malformed.

Before the Creation, there was no time. Time was created with the world. “Before” requires time. There is no before.

“Tempus autem non fuisset, nisi creatura fieret.”

“Time would not exist, unless a creature were made.”

This is a profound move: time is not the container in which events occur. Time is itself created — it is part of the universe, not the stage on which the universe performs.

Augustine anticipates, by 1500 years, Einstein’s insight that time and space are not prior to physics but are themselves physical.

The three presents:

Augustine’s most famous contribution to the philosophy of time:

The past does not exist — it is gone. The future does not exist — it has not yet come. The present is a knife-edge — it passes before you can name it.

And yet: we experience all three.

How?

Through the soul.

“Praesens de praeteritis memoria, praesens de praesentibus contuitus, praesens de futuris expectatio.”

“The present of past things is memory, the present of present things is sight, the present of future things is expectation.”

The three modes of time are three modes of the soul’s attention:

Memory — the past held in the present. Attention — the present grasped in the present. Expectation — the future anticipated in the present.

All three are present. All three are in the soul. Time is not out there in the world — it is in here, in the mind that holds past and future in its present attention.

“In te, anime meus, tempora metior.”

“In you, my soul, I measure time.”

This is Augustine’s radical claim: time is psychological. Not in the sense that it is unreal — but in the sense that temporal experience is constituted by the mind’s activity of holding past, present, and future together.

The distension of the soul:

Augustine uses the word distentio — distension, stretching — to describe the soul’s temporal structure.

The soul is stretched across time — pulled toward the past by memory, pulled toward the future by expectation, held at the present by attention.

This stretching is not comfortable. It is the source of anxiety — of the restlessness that Augustine describes in the Confessions’ most famous line:

“Inquietum est cor nostrum, donec requiescat in te.”

“Our heart is restless, until it rests in you.”

The restlessness is temporal. The soul stretched across past and future — unable to rest in the present — is the condition of temporal existence.

Rest — the Sabbath, eternity — is the release from distension.

II. Heidegger — Being-Toward-Death

Martin Heidegger’s Being and Time (1927) is the most ambitious philosophical work of the twentieth century — an attempt to ask, for the first time since the Greeks, the fundamental question:

What does it mean to be?

And his answer: to understand being, you must understand time. Because being is temporal. Everything that is — is in time.

Heidegger’s fundamental concept: Dasein — being-there — the kind of being that we are.

Dasein is not a subject contemplating an object. It is not a mind inside a body. It is being-in-the-world — already thrown into a situation, already caring about things, already engaged before reflection begins.

Thrownness:

We did not choose to exist. We did not choose our time, our place, our language, our body. We found ourselves thrown into a situation not of our choosing.

This is Geworfenheit — thrownness. The facticity of existence: that I am here, now, this — without having chosen any of it.

The temporality of Dasein:

Dasein is always: — Coming from a past it did not choose (thrownness) — Moving toward a future it cannot avoid (projection) — Existing in a present shaped by both

This is the temporal structure of human existence: ecstatic temporality — the “ek-stasis” (standing-outside) of existing always beyond the present moment, always relating to what was and what will be.

Being-toward-death:

Heidegger’s most radical claim:

Death is not an event that happens at the end. Death is a structure of existence — now.

I am always being-toward-death — always living in the light of my finitude, always existing as the being for whom my existence is an issue.

This is what distinguishes Dasein from a stone or a tree: not consciousness, exactly — but the fact that my being matters to me, that I must take a stand on it, that I cannot be indifferent to whether I exist.

Authentic and inauthentic existence:

Most of the time, we flee from the awareness of finitude.

We lose ourselves in the das Man — the “they,” the anonymous public — doing what one does, thinking what one thinks, living as one lives.

This is inauthenticity — not moral failure, but the everyday flight from the anxiety of genuine selfhood.

Authentic existence: confronting death, owning your thrownness, choosing from the possibilities that are genuinely yours — not because they are chosen for you by the anonymous they.

Anxiety:

For Heidegger, the mood that reveals the true structure of existence is Angst — anxiety.

Not fear of a specific thing. Anxiety is anxiety about nothing — about existence itself, about the groundlessness of being, about the fact that I am here without justification or guarantee.

Anxiety strips away the comfortable securities of everyday life and reveals what is always already there: the finite, temporal, groundless structure of human existence.

This is not pathology. It is clarity.

“Das Sein zum Tode enthüllt eine eigenste Möglichkeit des Daseins.”

“Being-toward-death discloses the ownmost possibility of Dasein.”

III. The Physics of Time — What Science Says

Philosophy asks: what is time? Physics answers — partially, strangely, in ways that deepen the mystery rather than resolve it.

Newton’s absolute time:

For Newton, time is absolute, true, and mathematical — flowing equably, without relation to anything external.

It is the background — the container — in which events occur.

The same time passes for everyone, everywhere. There is a universal now.

This is what common sense tells us. It is wrong.

Special relativity — the relativity of simultaneity:

Einstein showed (1905): simultaneity is relative. Two events simultaneous in one reference frame are not simultaneous in another.

There is no universal now. The “same moment” means nothing without specifying a reference frame.

Time dilation: moving clocks run slow. A clock on a fast-moving spaceship ticks more slowly than a clock at rest. Not as an illusion — really. The astronaut who travels at near-light-speed ages more slowly than the person who stays.

Time is not absolute. It is woven into spacetime — a four-dimensional manifold in which time and space are related by the Lorentz transformation.

General relativity — time curves:

In the presence of mass and energy, spacetime curves. Time itself curves.

Gravitational time dilation: clocks run slower in stronger gravitational fields. A clock at sea level ticks more slowly than a clock on a mountain.

Not by much — but measurably. GPS satellites must account for this.

Near a black hole: time slows toward the event horizon. At the horizon: time stops — from the perspective of a distant observer.

The block universe:

The most radical implication of relativity:

The block universe — or eternalism — the view that past, present, and future all equally exist.

In special relativity, different observers disagree about which events are simultaneous. There is no preferred “now” that carves the four-dimensional spacetime into past and future.

If there is no preferred now — if “now” is relative to the observer — then the distinction between “things that exist (present)” and “things that no longer exist (past)” is also observer-dependent.

The most natural interpretation: all moments equally exist. The past still exists. The future already exists.

We experience time as flowing — as having a present, a past, a future — but this experience may not correspond to the structure of the world.

The world may be a static four-dimensional block. The flow of time may be an illusion — a feature of our experience, not a feature of reality.

The arrow of time:

If the laws of physics are time-symmetric — if the equations of QM and GR look the same run forwards and backwards —

why does time have a direction?

Why do we remember the past but not the future? Why do broken eggs not reassemble? Why does entropy increase?

The arrow of time comes from: the second law of thermodynamics — entropy increases toward the future.

But this requires explanation: why was entropy low in the past?

The answer: the Big Bang was an extremely low-entropy state. The universe began in an improbable configuration. Everything since has been moving toward greater entropy — toward greater disorder — and this is the direction we call “future.”

The arrow of time points toward increasing entropy — and entropy was low at the beginning because of initial conditions we cannot explain from within the physics.

The Planck time:

At \(t_P \approx 5.39 \times 10^{-44}\) seconds — the Planck time — our physics breaks down.

Before the Planck time after the Big Bang: the concept of time may not apply.

Quantum gravity — which we do not have — is needed to describe what “time” means at the Planck scale.

Whether time is continuous or discrete, whether it had a beginning, whether it will have an end — these are questions our physics cannot currently answer.

IV. The Experience of Time — Phenomenology

Between Augustine’s psychological time and physics’ geometrical time, there is a third perspective: the phenomenology of temporal experience.

Husserl’s time-consciousness:

Edmund Husserl — Heidegger’s teacher — analyzed the structure of the “living present” in On the Phenomenology of the Consciousness of Internal Time (1928).

The present moment, for Husserl, is not a knife-edge — an instantaneous now with no duration.

It is a specious present — a small stretch of time that is experienced as simultaneous.

Within this living present: retention — the just-past, still held in the present experience — and protention — the about-to-come, already anticipated.

When you hear a melody, you hear the notes as a sequence — not as isolated sounds. You hear them as a melody because the earlier notes are retained while the later notes are heard.

The present moment of hearing contains, within it, the retained past and the anticipated future.

This is why music is possible. This is why speech is possible. Without retention and protention, each moment would be completely isolated — no connection to what came before, no anticipation of what comes next.

Time-consciousness makes experience coherent.

The phenomenology of boredom:

Heidegger, in his 1929-30 lecture course The Fundamental Concepts of Metaphysics, analyzes boredom as a fundamental mood that reveals the structure of time.

When bored, time slows — conspicuously. You become aware of time’s passing precisely because it is not passing.

The structure revealed: time is normally transparent — we live through it without noticing it, absorbed in our activities.

Boredom makes time opaque — suddenly visible, heavy, resistant.

The bored person is held by time, unable to fill it, unable to move through it, exposed to its pure passing.

This is why boredom is so uncomfortable — it exposes the temporality of existence that absorption normally conceals.

The phenomenology of flow:

Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi’s concept of flow — the state of complete absorption in a challenging activity — is the opposite of boredom.

In flow: time disappears. The person is entirely in the present — no past, no future, no awareness of duration.

Athletes, musicians, programmers, writers in the state of deep work — all report the disappearance of time.

Two hours pass in what feels like twenty minutes. Or twenty minutes feel like two hours.

The experience of time’s duration is not measured by the clock — it is constituted by the quality of attention.

Full attention: time disappears. Empty attention: time expands unbearably.

V. Memory — The Past That Lives

Augustine said: the present of past things is memory.

Memory is not a recording. It is a reconstruction.

The reconstructive nature of memory:

Every time you remember something, you reconstruct it — from fragments, from expectations, from your current emotional state, from the story you have been telling about your own past.

The remembered past is not the actual past. It is a present construction of what the past was.

This is not a failure of memory. It is what memory is.

The past, as experienced in memory, is always filtered through the present self doing the remembering.

Proust:

Marcel Proust spent 3,000 pages exploring this in In Search of Lost Time —

the madeleine dipped in tea that suddenly releases a memory not as a record but as a full sensory re-experience —

involuntary memory — the past that comes unbidden, more vivid than voluntary recollection —

and the narrator’s realization that what he was searching for — lost time — could only be recovered in art, which makes the past permanent by making it the present of the reader.

“The true paradises are the paradises we have lost.”

The lost past is more real than the present — because in the present, we are too close to see it.

Only in memory, only in art that activates memory, does experience become fully itself.

The physics of memory:

Memory is encoded in synaptic connections — in the physical structure of the brain.

When you remember, neurons fire in patterns that recreate the original pattern.

But: each time you remember, the memory is slightly altered — reconsolidated with new information.

The memory of a memory is not the original experience. It is the memory of the last time you remembered it.

The past, neurologically, is continuously rewritten in the present.

VI. The Future — Expectation and Anxiety

Augustine: the present of future things is expectation.

The future does not exist. And yet we live oriented toward it.

Every action is directed toward a future state. Every plan assumes a future. Every hope, every fear, every intention — all are directed toward what is not yet.

Heidegger’s Entwurf — projection — the way Dasein always throws itself ahead toward possibilities.

We are not beings who have a future. We are beings who are always already projecting ourselves toward a future — who exist in the mode of the not-yet.

The tyranny of the future:

The future that concerns us most is the one we cannot plan for: death.

The awareness of mortality — explicit or implicit — shapes the entire temporal horizon of experience.

Everything is done in the light of finitude. Every choice involves the other choices that are thereby not made. Every moment contains the mortality that makes it this moment and not an infinite succession of others.

This is not morbidity. It is the condition of seriousness.

The person who genuinely lives in the awareness of finitude takes their time seriously — knows that this moment is not replaceable, that this conversation, this decision, this experience — will not come again.

The person who lives in denial of finitude can always defer — there will be time later, another chance, a tomorrow that is structurally identical to today.

Death cancels the infinite deferral. It makes the present the only time there is.

VII. The Eternal Present

Against the rushing of time — the Buddhist, the Stoic, the contemplative traditions of many cultures have proposed a different relationship with time:

The eternal present.

Not eternity as infinite duration — time going on forever.

But eternity as the depth of the present — the present moment experienced in its fullness, without the pull of past or future.

Marcus Aurelius:

“Confine yourself to the present.”

Not as a counsel of forgetfulness — but as a recognition that the past and future are not available to us.

Only the present is real. Only the present can be inhabited. The mind that is always elsewhere — in the regretted past, in the anxious future — is never where it is.

The physics of the present:

In special relativity: the present — the “now” — is relative. Different observers have different nows.

Is there any absolute present?

The light cone of an event: the set of all events that could be causally connected — by signals traveling at most at the speed of light.

The absolute present — the set of events spacelike separated from here-now — is not causally connected to here-now. It cannot influence it. It cannot be influenced by it.

In a precise physical sense: the only “present” that exists is the infinitesimally thin slice of the light cone vertex.

Everything else is past or future.

The eternal present of contemplative traditions — the depth of the now — corresponds, oddly, to the physically real present: the knife-edge of the light cone.

What the mystic reaches for in meditation — the nowness of now — is, physically, the only moment there is.

VIII. Time and Language

Wittgenstein noted: the grammar of our language generates philosophical puzzles about time.

“Time passes” — but what is the subject? What is doing the passing?

“The same moment” — what makes moments the same or different?

“Now” — the most indexical word in language, pointing to the present — cannot be given a description that is not itself indexical.

Every attempt to describe “now” uses another word that refers to now.

The grammar of temporal language is not a description of time. It is a form of life — a set of practices for coordinating human activity in time.

When we ask “what is time?” we may be asking: what is the grammar of temporal language?

And the answer may be: there is no deeper answer. The grammar is what it is. The practices are what they are.

Time is what we do with it.

IX. What We Don’t Know

After Augustine, after Heidegger, after Einstein, after quantum mechanics —

what do we know about time?

We know:

Time dilates with velocity and gravity. The past and future are equally real in the block universe interpretation. The arrow of time comes from entropy. Time had a beginning — the Big Bang. Below the Planck time, time has no meaning.

We don’t know:

Whether time flows or is static. Whether the present is real or relative. Whether time is continuous or discrete. Whether time had a beginning in any absolute sense. What time was “before” the Big Bang — or whether “before” applies. Why time has a direction at all. Whether time is fundamental or emergent.

The deepest mystery:

Why does time seem to flow?

In the block universe, all moments equally exist. Nothing moves. Nothing passes. The universe is a static four-dimensional structure.

And yet: you experience time as flowing. The present feels different from the past. The future feels open in a way the past does not.

Why?

This question — the question of why we experience time as flowing in a world where physics suggests nothing flows —

is the hardest question in the philosophy of time.

It connects physics to consciousness. It connects the structure of the world to the structure of experience. It has no agreed answer.

X. Living in Time

All of this philosophy, all of this physics — and time remains what it was before any of it:

The medium of your life.

The stuff you are made of. The dimension in which everything happens — everything you remember, everything you anticipate, everything you are doing right now.

Augustine’s restless heart. Heidegger’s being-toward-death. Einstein’s dilating clocks. Proust’s involuntary memory. The Stoic’s present moment.

All of these are attempts to understand the same thing:

What it is to be a being who exists in time — who is constituted by past, oriented toward future, and always already in the present that passes before it can be held.

The answer is not available. The question is indispensable.

“Si nemo ex me quaerat, scio.”

If no one asks me, I know.

I know it right now — this moment, this passing, this movement from what was to what will be through the knife-edge of what is.

I know it in the way I know how to breathe.

And when I try to explain it —

I do not know.

Further reading: Augustine — Confessions (397) (trans. Henry Chadwick — Oxford World’s Classics) — Book XI for the philosophy of time Heidegger — Being and Time (1927) (trans. Macquarrie & Robinson, or Stambaugh) — Division Two for temporality and death Husserl — On the Phenomenology of the Consciousness of Internal Time (1928) (trans. Brough — Kluwer) — the technical foundation McTaggart — “The Unreality of Time” (1908) Mind 17, 457 — the A-series/B-series distinction; essential Price — Time’s Arrow and Archimedes’ Point (1996) Oxford University Press — the physics of time’s direction Barbour — The End of Time (1999) Oxford University Press — the block universe; time as illusion Rovelli — The Order of Time (2018) — the most beautiful popular account Proust — In Search of Lost Time (trans. Moncrieff & Kilmartin) — 3,000 pages on memory and time; worth every one For the physics: Price — Time’s Arrow (above) Penrose — The Road to Reality (2004) — Chapters 27-28 for time asymmetry
entry.summary: The most intimate and most mysterious of all phenomena. We live inside it. We cannot step outside it to examine it. And the closer we look, the stranger it becomes.

entry.title: Bivariate Bicycle Codes — A New Hope for qLDPC

link.title: Bivariate Bicycle Codes — A New Hope for qLDPC

entry.content:
qLDPC codes have been theoretically promising for years. The question was always: can we find explicit constructions with good parameters and tractable decoding and some hope of physical implementation?

Bivariate Bicycle (BB) codes, introduced by Bravyi et al. (2024), are a serious answer to that question.

Construction

BB codes are defined over the group algebra of \(\mathbb{Z}_l \times \mathbb{Z}_m\).

Let \(x\) and \(y\) be the cyclic shift operators on \(\mathbb{Z}_l\) and \(\mathbb{Z}_m\) respectively. Choose two polynomials:

\[A(x,y), \quad B(x,y) \in \mathbb{F}_2[x,y] / (x^l - 1, y^m - 1)\]

The parity check matrices are:

\[H_X = [A \mid B], \qquad H_Z = [B^T \mid A^T]\]

This gives a CSS code on \(n = 2lm\) physical qubits.

The commutativity condition \(H_X H_Z^T = 0\) is automatically satisfied by construction — a clean algebraic consequence of the bicyclic structure.

Why BB Codes Are Interesting

1. High encoding rate.

Surface code encodes \(k=1\) logical qubit into \(n \sim d^2\) physical qubits. BB codes achieve \(k = 2 \cdot \text{rank}(\ker A \cap \ker B^T)\), which for well-chosen polynomials gives \(k \sim \Theta(n)\).

The landmark example from Bravyi et al.: [[144, 12, 12]] — 144 physical qubits encoding 12 logical qubits at distance 12. Surface code needs ~1728 physical qubits for the same parameters.

2. Low-weight stabilizers.

All stabilizers have weight exactly 6 — three from \(A\), three from \(B\). This is a hard constraint from the polynomial structure, and it’s what makes BB codes physically plausible. Compare to hypergraph product codes where stabilizer weights grow with \(n\).

3. Quasi-cyclic structure.

The cyclic structure enables efficient syndrome extraction circuits and makes BP-OSD decoding particularly effective on these codes.

The Polynomial Choice Problem

Here’s what Bravyi et al. didn’t fully answer:

Given a group \(\mathbb{Z}_l \times \mathbb{Z}_m\), which polynomial pairs \((A, B)\) maximize the distance \(d\)?

Shaw & Terhal (2024) explored this more systematically, introducing the morphing circuit framework and identifying high-performance BB codes in several group sizes.

But their search was not exhaustive. The polynomial space is large, and distance computation is expensive — exact distance for a [[72, 12, ?]] code requires solving a minimum-weight codeword problem that is NP-hard in general.

So the question remains open: Are there BB codes with better parameters than currently known?

What I’m Working On

Systematic search in groups Shaw & Terhal didn’t fully explore.

For \(\mathbb{Z}_{12} \times \mathbb{Z}_6\) — \(n = 144\) physical qubits — I’ve been running exhaustive searches over polynomial pairs that include cross-terms: \(x^i y^j\) monomials Shaw restricted away.

Early results: several [[72, 12, 7]] codes with polynomial choices outside Shaw’s search space.

The trade-off: cross-term polynomials yield stabilizer weight 11 instead of Shaw’s weight 9 — more CNOT gates, harder circuits. Whether the distance gain justifies the circuit cost depends on the physical error model.

That’s the next question.

More soon. 🙂
entry.summary: Structure, parameters, and why they matter.

entry.title: The Art of Folding — Paper, History, and the Mathematics of Modular Origami

link.title: The Art of Folding — Paper, History, and the Mathematics of Modular Origami

entry.content:
There is a moment, when folding paper, where something shifts.

The material stops being passive. It begins to speak — in angles, in tensions, in the geometry of a crease that, once made, cannot be entirely unmade.

I have been folding since I was a child. Modular origami — the kind where dozens or hundreds of units interlock without glue into a single structure — is the form I keep returning to.

Not because it is the most beautiful. Not always. But because it is the most honest: every unit must be identical, every join must be precise, and the whole will not hold unless everything is right.

There is no hiding in modular origami.

I. The Invention of Paper

To understand origami, you must first understand paper.

Before paper, writing surfaces were expensive, heavy, fragile, or scarce. Clay tablets. Papyrus. Silk. Bamboo strips. None of these fold.

Paper — thin, flexible, cheap, strong — changed everything.

The invention is attributed to Cai Lun (蔡倫), a court official of the Han dynasty, who in 105 CE presented Emperor He with a new writing material made from bark, hemp, rags, and fishnets.

This is the traditional account. Archaeological evidence suggests papermaking existed in China perhaps two centuries earlier — fragments have been found dating to the Western Han period (206 BCE – 9 CE). But Cai Lun standardized and improved the process, and the court’s adoption of his method spread it throughout the empire.

The process he developed is recognizably modern:

Fibrous plant material — bark of mulberry, hemp, bamboo — is soaked, beaten into a pulp, suspended in water, and scooped onto a flat screen. As the water drains, the fibers bond. The sheet is pressed and dried.

The result: a material that is simultaneously strong and flexible, light and durable, and — crucially for origami — capable of holding a crease.

II. Paper Travels West

From China, papermaking spread along trade routes with extraordinary slowness by modern standards — and extraordinary speed by ancient ones.

Korea received papermaking in the 4th century CE. Korean paper — hanji (한지) — made from the bark of the mulberry tree (Broussonetia papyrifera) became famous for its exceptional durability. Documents written on hanji have survived a thousand years.

Japan received the technology in 610 CE, according to traditional accounts via the Buddhist monk Damjing from Korea. Japanese papermaking developed into an art form of its own — producing papers of extraordinary variety, texture, and beauty.

The Islamic world acquired papermaking after the Battle of Talas (751 CE), where Arab forces captured Chinese papermakers. Paper mills appeared in Samarkand, then Baghdad, then across the Islamic world. The Arabic word for paper — waraq — entered dozens of languages.

Europe received paper through the Islamic world, with the first European paper mills appearing in Spain and Italy in the 12th and 13th centuries.

By the time Gutenberg invented movable type in 1440, paper was ready for it.

III. Japanese Paper — Washi

The Japanese word for paper is kami (紙). It shares its sound — though not its character — with the word for spirit or god: kami (神).

This is not coincidence, or rather, it is the kind of coincidence that shapes a culture. Paper in Japan was sacred material. It was used in Shinto rituals, folded into ritual forms called shide that still hang in shrines today.

Japanese papermaking — washi (和紙, wa = Japanese, shi = paper) — developed its own distinct tradition.

The finest washi papers are still made by hand, using traditional techniques that have changed little in a thousand years.

Among the most celebrated:

Washi (和紙) — the general term for traditional Japanese handmade paper, made primarily from three plants: kōzo (楮, mulberry), mitsumata (三椏), and gampi (雁皮). Each produces paper with distinct qualities — kōzo is strong and flexible, gampi is smooth and lustrous.

Yuzen (友禅) — not strictly a paper type but a dyeing technique, originally developed for silk kimono fabric in the Edo period by the fan painter Miyazaki Yūzen. The technique — using rice paste as a resist to create precise, elaborate patterns — was later applied to paper. Yuzen paper is used in bookbinding, crafts, and high-end origami.

Chiyogami (千代紙) — chiyо means “thousand generations,” gami is the voiced form of kami (paper). Traditional Japanese decorative paper, featuring bold, repetitive patterns derived from kimono fabric designs. Originally produced in Kyoto and Edo. The patterns — cranes, waves, cherry blossoms, geometric forms — are printed using hand-carved woodblocks. Used in traditional crafts and ceremonial wrapping.

Kozo (楮紙) — pure mulberry paper, prized for strength. Used in conservation and restoration of artworks worldwide. The Library of Congress uses kozo paper to repair ancient documents.

Momigami (揉み紙) — paper that has been crumpled and worked by hand until it achieves a soft, leather-like texture. Used in decorative arts and certain origami techniques.

IV. The Word Origami

Origami (折り紙) — ori (折り) meaning “folding,” kami (紙) meaning “paper” — voiced to gami in compound.

The word is surprisingly recent.

The practice of paper folding in Japan is ancient — at least as old as the introduction of paper itself. But for most of Japanese history, the craft was called orikata (折り形, “folded shapes”) or orisue (折り据え).

The word origami in its current sense became standard only in the modern period — some scholars date its widespread use to the Meiji era (1868–1912) or even later.

Before this, two distinct traditions existed:

Noshi (熨斗) — formal paper folding for ceremonial use. Paper folded around gifts, used in wedding ceremonies, attached to sake cups. These were rigid, prescribed forms — not art but ritual.

Tsutsumi (包み) — wrapping and folding paper for gifts and formal presentation. The aesthetics of wrapping — the geometry of folded paper — were codified into elaborate systems of etiquette.

The tradition of folding paper for play and artistic expression — closer to what we now call origami — developed alongside these ceremonial forms and was documented in the 18th century work Hiden Senbazuru Orikata (秘伝千羽鶴折形, 1797): “The Secret of One Thousand Cranes” — the first known book of origami instructions.

V. The Crane and the Legend

Senbazuru — one thousand cranes.

The tradition: fold one thousand paper cranes, and your wish will be granted. Some versions say: you will be healed of illness. Some say: you will live for a thousand years.

The legend became inseparable from Sadako Sasaki — a girl who was two years old when the atomic bomb fell on Hiroshima in 1945.

She developed leukemia at twelve. She began folding cranes in the hospital, aiming for one thousand.

She folded 644 before she died. Her classmates completed the remaining 356.

She was twelve years old.

There is a statue of Sadako in the Hiroshima Peace Memorial Park. She holds a golden crane. Every year, thousands of children from around the world send origami cranes to be placed at her memorial.

This is what paper can hold.

VI. The Mathematics of Folding

For most of its history, origami was transmitted as craft — through instruction, imitation, practice.

In the 20th century, mathematicians began to ask: what are the underlying principles?

The result was a new field: computational origami — the mathematics of paper folding.

Huzita-Hatori axioms:

In 1989, Humiaki Huzita formalized the operations possible with a single fold. Later extended by Koshiro Hatori, the complete set of seven axioms defines what can be constructed by folding.

Remarkably: origami is more powerful than compass-and-straightedge construction. With origami, you can:

  • Trisect an angle (impossible with compass and straightedge)
  • Double a cube (impossible with compass and straightedge)
  • Solve certain cubic equations

The fold is a more powerful geometric operation than the compass arc or the straight line.

Flat-foldability:

When does a crease pattern fold flat?

Two necessary and sufficient conditions for a single vertex:

  1. Kawasaki’s theorem: At any interior vertex, the alternating sum of angles equals zero: \(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 + \alpha_3 - \alpha_4 + \cdots = 0\)

  2. Maekawa’s theorem: At any interior vertex, the number of mountain folds and valley folds differ by exactly two: \(|M - V| = 2\)
